narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid but not standout academic trajectory, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles like yours from past students. The model estimated a 60% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed right away to maintain their strong bar passage rates. This decision hinged primarily on three key factors from your profile: your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and Year 3 school decile ranking. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.20—noticeably below the typical 3.21 average—stood out as the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a foundational academic challenge that often correlates with bar struggles. Next, your LSAT score of 31, well under the average of 36.7, further reinforced this risk by suggesting potential difficulties with the intense analytical demands of law school and the bar. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile of 5—slightly better than the average 5.53—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success and showing some improvement over time. Compared to typical students who pass, your lower GPA and LSAT placed you in a tougher spot, while your full-time enrollment plans aligned with the vast majority (over 92%) who succeed that way, and your lower-middle family income bracket was common but not a major factor here. The model weighed these elements together, with the gaps in GPA and LSAT outweighing your later decile gains. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as even a few points could shift your profile significantly. Strengthening your overall academic narrative through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate growth beyond your undergraduate record. Many applicants in similar situations reapply successfully after targeted improvements, so reviewing your full application with an advisor could pinpoint other edges. With persistence, you can reshape how models like this see your potential.
